description: Collection of late Nepalese manuscripts used by Conze in his critical edition. These manuscripts (11th-19th century) represent the elaborated Tantric recension with features absent from earlier witnesses.

manuscripts.conze_description: Nepalese manuscript, Cambridge University Library Add. 1164
manuscripts.notes: One of the earlier Nepalese witnesses

manuscripts.conze_description: Nepalese manuscript, Cambridge University Library Add. 1165
manuscripts.notes: Extended version with frame narrative. Includes oṃ in mantra.

manuscripts.conze_description: Nepalese manuscript, Royal Asiatic Society, Hodgson Collection
manuscripts.notes: Late manuscript with full Tantric elaborations

manuscripts.conze_description: Nepalese manuscript, Nepal-German Manuscript Preservation Project

general_characteristics.recension: Mostly long (extended) version with frame narrative
general_characteristics.significance: Represent late elaboration of text with Tantric additions; contrast with earlier witnesses like Hōryūji

mangala.comparison: Elaborate Tantric salutation absent from Chinese texts, T256, and Hōryūji (which has simple 'namas sarvajñāya')
mangala.note: The address to Prajñāpāramitā as a feminine deity (Bhagavatyai) and inclusion of Oṃ reflects Tantric influence

om_in_mantra.nepalese_reading: Oṃ gate gate pāragate pārasaṃgate bodhi svāhā
om_in_mantra.comparison: Hōryūji and most other witnesses lack oṃ; only Nb and Ne among Conze's witnesses include it
om_in_mantra.note: Addition of oṃ reflects Tantric convention that all mantras begin with oṃ (Attwood 2021)

title_variants.note: Nepalese manuscripts show considerable variation in titles; the 'Pañcaviṃśatikā' prefix references a syllable count (25 × 32 = 800 syllables)

frame_narrative.description: Long recension manuscripts include prologue (nidāna) and epilogue with Buddha on Vulture Peak, Śāriputra's question prompted by Avalokiteśvara, and audience approval
frame_narrative.note: Frame narrative matches Tibetan long version but absent from Chinese T251 and Sanskrit short recension

prologue.iast: evaṃ mayā śrutam ekasmin samaye bhagavān rājagṛhe viharati sma gṛdhrakūṭe parvate mahatā bhikṣusaṃghena sārdhaṃ mahatā ca bodhisattvasaṃghena | tena khalu samayena bhagavān gambhīrāvabhāsaṃ nāma samādhiṃ samāpanno 'bhūt | tena ca samayena āryāvalokiteśvaro bodhisattvo mahāsattvo gambhīrāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caryāṃ caramāṇo vyavalokayati sma...
prologue.english: Thus have I heard. At one time the Blessed One was dwelling at Rājagṛha on Vulture Peak Mountain together with a great assembly of monks and a great assembly of bodhisattvas. At that time the Blessed One had entered a samādhi called 'Profound Illumination.' And at that time Noble Avalokiteśvara, the bodhisattva mahāsattva, while practicing the profound prajñāpāramitā, was examining...

epilogue.iast: ...iti āryāvalokiteśvaro bodhisattvo mahāsattvo 'bhāṣata | atha khalu bhagavān tasmāt samādher vyutthāya āryāvalokiteśvarasya bodhisattvasya mahāsattvasya sādhu-kāram adāt | sādhu sādhu kulaputra evam etat kulaputra evam etat gambhīrāyāṃ prajñāpāramitāyāṃ caryāṃ cartavyam...
epilogue.english: ...Thus spoke Noble Avalokiteśvara, the bodhisattva mahāsattva. Then the Blessed One arose from that samādhi and gave his approval to Noble Avalokiteśvara the bodhisattva mahāsattva: 'Excellent, excellent, son of good family! It is so, son of good family, it is so. The practice of the profound prajñāpāramitā should be practiced...'

section_I.nepalese: Manuscripts vary; most have nominative following received tradition

section_VI.issue: Full stop placement and niṣṭhānirvāṇaḥ vs niṣṭhānirvāṇaprāptaḥ
section_VI.nepalese: Late manuscripts often have niṣṭhānirvāṇaprāptaḥ with wrongly placed full stop
section_VI.note: Conze's 1975 edition follows this late reading

na_aprapti.note: Absent from Hōryūji and Large Sutra parallels (Huifeng 2014)

stemmatic_position.summary: The Nepalese manuscripts represent a late, elaborated tradition that diverged significantly from the earlier transmission. They show influence of Tantric Buddhism and should not be privileged over earlier witnesses like the Hōryūji manuscript.
relationship_to_other_witnesses: Descended from Sanskrit tradition that was itself back-translated from Chinese
Elaborated with Tantric elements (oṃ, Bhagavatī maṅgala, frame narrative)
Titles referencing Pañcaviṃśatikā suggest awareness of Large Sutra origins
Late readings (na-aprāptiḥ, niṣṭhānirvāṇaprāptaḥ) should not be preferred over earlier witnesses

scholarly_refs: Conze, Edward (1948). 'Text, Sources, and Bibliography of the Prajñāpāramitā-hṛdaya.' JRAS 80(1-2): 33-51.
Conze, Edward (1967). 'The Prajñāpāramitā-Hṛdaya Sūtra.' In Thirty Years of Buddhist Studies, pp. 147-167.
Watanabe, Shōgo (2016). 'A Comparative Study of the Pañcaviṃśatisāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā.' Tokyo.
Silk, Jonathan (1994). 'The Heart Sūtra in Tibetan: A Critical Edition of the Two Recensions.' Wiener Studien zur Tibetologie und Buddhismuskunde 34.
Attwood, Jayarava (2021). 'Revised Editions of the Prajñāpāramitāhṛdaya and the Xīnjīng.' Draft.
